title: practical english usage - Part 337
content:
express a future meaning (►  23 1 ).

In subordinate clauses after mind, a present tense is usually used if we  want to 5 tenses 'Do you mind if I look at your paper? 'No, please do / that's OK  / sure.' permission (but we  often add more words to make the meaning quite clear).

After Would/Do you mind..we  use No or Not at all (more formal) to give 4 answers or (   .. . if I smoke?) Do you mind my smoking? (More informal:... me smoking?

after mind (►  94.3-94.4).

In a slightly more formal style, we  can use my, your, etc with an -ing form 3 Do/Would         you     mind      my   .. .ing?

Would you mind if I opened the window?

Would you mind opening the window? (= Please open ...) permission.

We   can use Would you mind.. .? to ask people to do things, or to ask for 2  Would       you     mind     .. .?

I don't mind you coming in late if you don't wake me up.

Do you mind waiting a few minutes? (     ... to wait...) After mind, we  can use an -ing form, or object + -ing form.

Do you mind if we leave a bit earlier today?  I don't mind if you use my car.

'Do you mind the smell of tobacco? 'Not at all.' questions and negative clauses.

Mind can mean ‘ dislike', ‘ be annoyed by’ , ‘ object to'. We   use mind mostly in 1  meaning       and   use 528 mind:     do   you     mind,      etc Galileo was by no means the first person to use a telescope.

'Is that all you've got to say? 'By no means.' similar to definitely not, or not by a long way.

By no means (or not by any means) is not the opposite of by all means. It is Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 530 vocabulary •         name My name's Smith. I have an appointment with Mr Andrews.

'Hi. Come in. What's your name.* 'Jenny.' 'Name, please? 'Alan Bennett.' only one part may be used, depending on the situation.

The  word name does not mean just one or other part of this - though of course Andy Barlow, William Shakespeare, Carol Jane Griffiths, Naseem Khan, Li Wei.

A person's name is the whole expression that identifies them - for example 530 name 495 conjunction                ► the    moment: He had several teeth missing.

We've got some plates missing - do you think Luke's borrowed them?

In an informal style, a structure with have... missing is also possible.

There's a page missing from this book.

there is.

We   can use missing after a noun. This often happens in clauses beginning with The missing children were found at their aunt's house.

When did you realise that the money was missing?

Missing is often used as an adjective, meaning ‘ lost’ .

I am sorry that lack of time prevents me from giving more details.

. .. they lack electricity.

In a formal style, the verb or noun lack can be used to express this idea.

In some of the villages they haven't got electricity, (     ... they miss Miss is not used simply to say that somebody has not got something.

4 miss     not   used The child ran away in the morning but nobody missed her for hours.

Another meaning of miss is 'notice that somebody/something is not there’ .

3 'notice     the   absence      of' I regret working with you. (= I’ m  sorry ɪ  was with you.) I miss working with you. (= I’ m  sorry I'm no longer with you.) Note that regret is not used in the same way. Compare: He's not happy in the country - he misses city life.

Will you miss me when I'm away?

somebody, or no longer have something.

We   can use miss to say that we  are sorry because we  are no longer with 2 'be  sorry    to  be   without' I got in too late and missed seeing the news on TV.

An -ing form can be used after miss.

vocabulary •  532  need possible in questions, after if, and in other ‘ non-affirmative' structures (►  222). -> These forms are used mainly in negative sentences (needn't); but they are also She needn't reserve a seat - there'll be plenty of room.

without do. In this case, need is normally followed by an infinitive without to.

the third person singular has no -s, and questions and negatives are made Need can also have the same present-tense forms as modal auxiliary verbs: 2 modal     auxiliary       forms:    he   needn't;       need      I?
